question: What is the primary advantage of Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS) over a standard Depth-First Search?
expectedPoints: IDDFS combines the memory efficiency of Depth-First Search (DFS)
with the completeness and optimality of Breadth-First Search (BFS) for unweighted graphs
It finds the shallowest goal node first by gradually increasing the depth limit

question: Explain the process of Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search and why its time complexity is still considered acceptable despite repeated expansions.
expectedPoints: IDDFS performs a series of DFS searches with increasing depth limits (e.g., depth = 0, then 1, then 2, etc.) until a goal is found
Nodes at the final level (d) are expanded once, nodes at level d-1 are expanded twice, and so on
The overhead of repeated expansions is minor because the number of nodes grows exponentially with depth, so most nodes are at the deepest level

question: Compare and contrast Iterative Deepening DFS, Breadth-First Search, and Depth-First Search. Discuss their completeness, optimality, and space and time complexities, providing a scenario where IDDFS would be the most suitable algorithm.
expectedPoints: Completeness: BFS and IDDFS are complete for finite graphs; DFS is not
Optimality: BFS and IDDFS are optimal for unweighted graphs; DFS is not
Space Complexity: DFS and IDDFS have O(bd) space; BFS has O(b^d) space, which is a major disadvantage
Time Complexity: All are O(b^d) in worst-case time
Scenario: IDDFS is most suitable when the search space is large, the solution depth is unknown, and memory is limited (e.g., searching a very deep tree on a system with constrained RAM)
